original_image_tags: black-and-white photograph of a night sky with a light-colored, disc-shaped object indicated by a drawn arrow.
black-and-white photograph of the Vought V-173 "Flying Pancake" aircraft on the ground.
head-and-shoulders newspaper portrait photograph of a man.

elements.description: A vertical photograph of a man in a suit looking directly at the camera. The caption reads: 'Post Staff Photo. DAVID ATAMIAN, of 5160 Shoemaker la., Friendship Heights, Md., said he saw three or four flying discs Friday about midnight'.

A photograph showing a dark, blurry, saucer-shaped object in a cloudy sky with a white arrow pointing to it. The caption reads: 'Associated Press WIREPHOTO. IS IT?—Coast Guardsman Frank Ryman, 27, made this picture with a Speed Graphic camera from the front porch of his Seattle, Wash., residence. It shows, says Ryan, a “white saucer” (arrow) that is neither an airplane, a cloud, nor a silver balloon. So, is it a “flapjack” or a saucer? Or maybe a bottle cap? Photo is enlarged about 20 times'.

A photograph of a dark, circular, wingless aircraft (the Vought V-173 'Flying Pancake') on the ground. The caption reads: 'THIS IS NOT ONE OF 'EM, SAYS THE NAVY—The “flying pancake,” a wingless plane developed by the Navy, is the only plane it has which might resemble the reported “flying saucers,” but officials in Washington said yesterday that this strange-looking aircraft has never left Bridgeport, Conn. Authorities are still skeptical that the mystery missiles said to have been seen in the skies over the West Coast and as far East as the Carolinas are any sort of new airplane'.

judge.reason: Description A lists three visual elements (a night sky photo, an aircraft photo, and a portrait photo) while Description B describes three distinct newspaper clipping illustrations with detailed captions, but the specific quoted caption text in B (e.g., the man's name, location, and aircraft details) is entirely absent from A, which is a different set of visual content.
